1: The Servant calls to the coastlands and distant peoples to listen — YHWH called him from the womb and named him before birth. Prenatal divine commissioning is a mark of prophetic legitimacy (Jeremiah 1:5) but here the scale exceeds any individual prophet: the audience is the entire known world, from Mediterranean coastlands to eastern nations. This is the Second Servant Song (Isaiah 49:1–6).
2: He made my mouth like a sharp sword and hid me in the shadow of his hand — a polished arrow concealed in his quiver. The hidden weapon imagery reflects ANE military practice of concealing elite troops until the strategic moment. The Servant's word is both sword and precision arrow — instruments of conquest without conventional warfare.
3: He said to me, You are my servant, Israel, in whom I will be glorified. The Servant is named Israel — a point of intense interpretive debate in Second Temple Judaism and Christianity. Whether corporate Israel or an individual representative who embodies Israel's vocation, the identification grounds the Servant's mission in covenant identity.
4: But I said, I have labored in vain — I have spent my strength for nothing and vanity, yet my cause is with YHWH and my reward with my God. The lament of apparent futility (tohu, the same word as Genesis 1:2 formless void) paradoxically trusts YHWH with the outcome. This mirrors the psalms of lament where the petitioner brings honest failure to the covenant lord.
5: YHWH formed me from the womb to be his servant — to bring Jacob back to him, and Israel would be gathered to him. The Servant's commission begins as a restoration task for ethnic Israel, consistent with the standard prophetic call. The honor is not diminished by apparent failure; YHWH is his strength.
6: It is too small a thing that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob — I will make you a light for the nations so that my salvation may reach the ends of the earth. The commission's expansion from Israel-restoration to universal light-for-nations (goyim) is the theological breakthrough. The Servant's scope exceeds any prior prophetic role. Paul quotes this verse (Acts 13:47) as scriptural basis for Gentile mission.
7: Thus says YHWH, the Redeemer of Israel, his Holy One, to one deeply despised, abhorred by the nation, the servant of rulers: Kings shall see and arise, princes and they shall prostrate themselves. The reversal pattern — abject humiliation followed by royal prostration — is standard ANE royal theology inverted. It is the shamed servant, not the powerful ruler, who commands international honor.
8: In a time of favor I have answered you — in a day of salvation I have helped you. Paul quotes this verse directly in 2 Corinthians 6:2, applying it to the present eschatological moment. The phrase time of favor (et ratzon) is a technical term for the acceptable, appointed period when YHWH's disposition toward the petitioner is favorable — a window to be seized.
9: Saying to the prisoners, Come out — to those in darkness, Show yourselves. They shall feed along the ways and on all bare heights shall be their pasture. The prisoner-release announcement echoes the Jubilee year (Lev 25), when debt-slaves were freed. The pastoral imagery (feeding along roads) reimagines the return from exile as an Edenic provision — the wilderness itself becomes a feeding ground.
10: They shall not hunger or thirst, neither scorching wind nor sun shall strike them — for he who has compassion on them will lead them and guide them to springs of water. The wilderness-provision motif continues. The compassion-shepherd who leads to water springs inverts the death-by-exposure fate of refugees crossing desert terrain — the threat becomes the site of miraculous provision.
11: I will make all my mountains a road and my highways shall be raised up. The cosmic road-building project recapitulates the new-Exodus highway of Isaiah 40:3–4 — valleys exalted, mountains lowered. In ANE royal ideology, road-building demonstrated imperial power; YHWH surpasses all empires by reshaping geography itself.
12: Behold, these shall come from afar — from the north and from the west and from the land of Sinim. The ingathering from Sinim (possibly Aswan/Egypt or a far eastern region) extends the return beyond Babylon to the entire diaspora. The universal ingathering of scattered Israel from all compass points is a restored-Eden vision.
13: Sing for joy, O heavens, and exult, O earth — break forth into singing, O mountains! For YHWH has comforted his people and will have compassion on his afflicted. The cosmic chorus (heaven, earth, mountains) is the creation's response to covenant renewal. ANE hymns regularly called on the natural order to celebrate divine victories; here creation celebrates Israel's redemption.
14: But Zion said, YHWH has forsaken me — my Lord has forgotten me. The lament of abandonment uses the strongest possible covenant-failure language: forsaken (azav) and forgotten (shakach) are the covenant curses of Deuteronomy. Zion personified voices the communal experience of exile as divine rejection.
15: Can a woman forget her nursing child — have no compassion on the son of her womb? Even these may forget, yet I will not forget you. The mother-nursing-infant image is the most intimate possible attachment in the ANE household. The rhetorical question sets the emotional stakes: even if the strongest biological bond could break, YHWH's memory of Zion cannot.
16: Behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands — your walls are continually before me. Engraving names on palms was not a common ANE practice, making the image startling: YHWH carries Jerusalem's name as a permanent bodily inscription. The walls of the destroyed city are always visible to him — he has not looked away.
17: Your builders come quickly — your destroyers and those who laid you waste go out from you. The reversal of Babylon's demolition of Jerusalem: the destroyers depart and builders rush in. This is the concrete logistics of return-and-rebuild, framed as YHWH's direct action.
18: Lift up your eyes and see — they all gather and come to you. As I live, declares YHWH, you shall put them all on as an ornament and bind them on as a bride does. The returning children are jewelry and bridal ornament — a striking image of communal identity restored. The oath formula As I live (chai ani) is a divine self-invocation, the strongest possible assurance in ANE discourse.
19: Your waste and your desolate places and your devastated land — surely now you will be too narrow for your inhabitants, and those who swallowed you will be far away. Overpopulation after desolation is the sign of blessing (cf. Genesis 26:22). The repopulation astonishment theme runs through the restoration oracles: more children than the land can hold.
20: The children born in the time of your bereavement will yet say in your hearing, The place is too narrow for me — give me a place to dwell in. The children of exile — born during national death — outlive the catastrophe and demand living space. The voice of the next generation claiming territory is the sound of covenant restoration.
21: Then you will say in your heart, Who has borne me these? I was bereaved and barren, exiled and put away — who brought these up? I was left alone; from where have these come? The astonishment-question pattern appears in ANE royal annals when a king's territory expands beyond expectation. Zion's bewilderment at her own restoration is the appropriate response to divine surprise.
22: Thus says the Lord YHWH: Behold, I will lift up my hand to the nations and raise my signal to the peoples — they shall bring your sons in their arms and your daughters shall be carried on their shoulders. Lifting the hand was the ANE gesture of oath-swearing and military signal. Nations become the transport fleet for Israel's return — reversal of the deportation that brought exiles to Babylon.
23: Kings shall be your foster fathers and their queens your nursing mothers — they shall bow down to you with their faces to the ground and lick the dust of your feet. The prostration-and-dust imagery is drawn from ANE royal submission iconography. Vassal kings prostrated to the great king and kissed his feet; here that gesture is redirected to Israel by the very kings who once dominated her.
24: Can the prey be taken from the mighty man, or the captive of a tyrant be rescued? The rhetorical question invokes the logic of power: once a conqueror holds captives, no one challenges him. The expected answer is no — making YHWH's rescue all the more astonishing when the next verse overturns it.
25: For thus says YHWH: Even the captives of the mighty shall be taken and the prey of the tyrant delivered — for I will contend with those who contend with you, and I will save your children. YHWH as Israel's divine warrior enters the legal-military contest on Israel's behalf. The verb contend (rib) is both legal and military — a covenant lawsuit combined with battle.
26: I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh and they shall be drunk with their own blood as with wine — then all flesh shall know that I, YHWH, am your Savior and your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob. The self-cannibalization image appears in ANE siege-warfare descriptions as the ultimate horror of defeat. Here the oppressors' destruction becomes the proof of YHWH's identity as Israel's kinsman-redeemer.
